title: Operating Cash Flows — Indirect Method
event: Reyes Co. reports net income of $60,000, depreciation expense of $14,000, and a $3,000 gain on the sale of equipment. During the year accounts receivable increased $5,000, inventory decreased $2,000, and accounts payable increased $4,000. Compute cash flows from operating activities (indirect method).

computationPaths.narration: Start with net income and undo everything non-cash: add back depreciation, strip out gains (they belong to investing), then translate accruals to cash via working-capital changes.

steps.label: AR up = cash not yet collected
AP up = cash not yet paid

memorize.body: Asset UP → cash DOWN. Liability UP → cash UP. Every working-capital change follows this.
Gains are SUBTRACTED and losses ADDED — the full sale proceeds already live in investing; leaving the gain in operating double-counts it.

questions.prompt: Using the facts above, cash from operating activities = ?

distractors.feedback: You ADDED the gain. Gains come out of operating income — the cash from that sale sits in investing: 72,000.
You forgot to add back depreciation — a noncash expense that reduced income but never touched cash.
